coredata.dc:title: Rapid trace analysis of ceftriaxone using new fluorescent carbon dots as a highly sensitive turn-off nanoprobe
coredata.dc:creator: Narimani, Saeedeh
Samadi, Naser
coredata.dc:description: Novel highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a green, low-cost, and simple hydrothermal method from the chicken as a carbon source. No surface passivating or chemical reagents were applied in the introduced synthesis method. The obtained CDs exhibited blue fluorescence emission with a high quantum yield (QY) of approximately 32.86% and noticeable water solubility. FT-IR and XPS measurements showed the presence of carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the CDs. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was remarkably declined in the presence of ceftriaxone (CTR) by the inner filter effect (IFE) process. Furthermore, the results showed the excellent selectivity of chicken-derived CDs toward CTR with satisfactory detection limit of 4.4 × 10−10 M and a linear range from 9 × 10−10 M to 1.4 × 10−8 M. The reliability of this method was validated by determining CTR in different spiked urine samples. It was concluded that the proposed method was simple, rapid, economical, and selective for the determination of CTR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ceftriaxone
Fluorescent
Chicken-derived carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesize of CDs from chicken products
Optimization of experimental conditions for the detection of CTR

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of the synthesized chicken drumstick-derived CDs, Inset: the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (A), Fluorescence 3D emission spectra of chicken drumstick-derived CDs under different excitation wavelengths (B) CDs: carbon dots.
FT-IR spectra of chicken drumstick-derived CDs, CTR and CDs + CTR. CDs: carbon dots; CTR: Ceftriaxone.
(a) XPS, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s spectra of the chicken drumstick-derived CDs. CDs: carbon dots.

Fluorescence emission spectra of chicken drumstick-derived CDs in the presence of various concentrations of CTR at buffer pH = 6.0. Inset: plot of (F0/F) vs. concentrations of CTR introduced. CDs: carbon dots; CTR: Ceftriaxone; F0 and F: fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in the absence and presence of Ceftriaxone, respectively.
The QY of CDs synthesized from different materials.
Comparison of the method with different determination CTR methods.
Determination of CTR in real samples using CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Samples 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 are from the Volunteer's human urine.

head.ce:title: Rapid trace analysis of ceftriaxone using new fluorescent carbon dots as a highly sensitive turn-off nanoprobe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a green, low-cost, and simple hydrothermal method from the chicken as a carbon source. No surface passivating or chemical reagents were applied in the introduced synthesis method. The obtained CDs exhibited blue fluorescence emission with a high quantum yield (QY) of approximately 32.86% and noticeable water solubility. FT-IR and XPS measurements showed the presence of carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the CDs. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was remarkably declined in the presence of ceftriaxone (CTR) by the inner filter effect (IFE) process. Furthermore, the results showed the excellent selectivity of chicken-derived CDs toward CTR with satisfactory detection limit of 4.4 × 10−10 M and a linear range from 9 × 10−10 M to 1.4 × 10−8 M. The reliability of this method was validated by determining CTR in different spiked urine samples. It was concluded that the proposed method was simple, rapid, economical, and selective for the determination of CTR.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new member of carbon-based nanomaterials that were synthesized in 2004. They have recently attracted wide attention because of their excellent stability, cost-effectiveness, water-solubility, low cytotoxicity, as well as high biocompatibility and flexibility in surface functionalization compared to organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [1–6]. Therefore, CDs are playing critical roles in various fields such as chemiluminescence [7–9], fluorescence sensing [10–15], and bioimaging [16,17]. There are many reports about the CDs synthesis, which are electrochemical oxidation [18], microwave-assisted methods [19,20], ultrasonication [21], and hydrothermal synthesis [22–24]. Recently CDs have also been reported to measure many important analytes, including drugs, metals, small molecules, and anions [25–27].

CTR (Figure S1), a semi-synthetic antibiotic, is a third-generation cephalosporin derivative used to treat bacterial infections caused by vulnerable and generally gram-positive organisms. The bactericidal activity of CTR is achieved by preventing bacteria from making a cell wall [28]. Multiple methods have been reported for detection of CTR such as capillary electrophoresis (CE) [29,30], high-performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC) [31], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [32–34]. These methods are expensive and complicated; therefore it is of extreme importance to find cost-effective and rapid methods for the determination of CTR. Fluorescence (FL) is a powerful tool in analytical determination because of its high sensitivity, low detection limit, and cost-effectiveness features. Recently some methods have been reported for the determination of CTR based on fluorescence [6,35,36]. However, in these articles, QDs have been mainly used for the determination of CTR. QDs, especially the cadmium-containing QDs, are highly toxic, and their preparation method is complex.

In this study, new CDs for the sensitive and selective determination of CTR were successfully synthesized using a simple, green, economical, and rapid hydrothermal method without using any chemical materials. The CDs were synthesized from the four-part of the chicken, which the chicken drumstick-derived CDs had high QY and showed more selectivity toward CTR determination, and were chosen for the trace analysis of CTR (Figure S2). Figure S3 is the synthesized schematic diagram of CDs.

CTR and other drugs were of analytical grade. Chemical reagents such as potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4), amino acids, quinine sulfate, and metal ions were obtained from Merck (Germany), and the chicken products were purchased from the local market.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize of CDs from chicken products
ce:section.ce:para: Simple hydrothermal treatment was employed to prepare chicken-product-derived CDs. Briefly, 25 g of chicken drumstick was cut into small pieces and mixed with 60 mL doubly distilled water. The mixture was then heated in a 150 mL stainless steel laboratory autoclave lined with teflon, at 190 °C for 5 h. After cooling the autoclave to 25 °C, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min to remove large agglomerated nanoparticles. The CDs solution was subsequently filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membranes and freeze-dried. Then, it was redispersed in double-distilled water for further use. CDs derived from chicken feet, chicken gizzard, and chicken breast were also synthesized using the same procedure described above.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were carried out on a Biochrom WPA Biowave II instrument. All Fluorescence emission spectra were measured with a JASCOFP 6500PC spectrofluorimeter. The Metrohm 827 PH-Lab was utilized for measuring the pH. CDs’ size and structure were correspondingly performed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, the Philips-CM 30 model with a voltage of 300 kV). The IR spectra were recorded by a nexus 670 FT- IR Thermo Nicolet spectrometer using KBr disk. Meanwhile, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of chicken drumstick CDs was obtained at a voltage of 40 kV with a Philips PW 1730 diffractometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed on a Specs-Flex XPS system with the energy step size of 200 eV-4KeV.

Fluorescence QY of all prepared CDs that were derived from different parts of the chicken were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm as follows (Eq.1):
(1)
Q
Y
x
= Q
Y
r
m
x
m
r
η
x
2
η
r
2
where ‘x’ represents CD, superscript ‘r’ refers to quinine sulfate, ‘QY’ is quantum yield, m refers to the slope of the standard curve of the quantum yield, and ‘η’ indicates the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate was also utilized as the standard (QY: 54.6%) through its dissolving in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and the absorbance was kept below 0.1 The CDs were dissolved in double-distilled water.

For CTR detection, 1 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0) was added to 2 mL of 0.15 mg/mL CDs. Then an appropriate concentration of CTR solution was added to the solution, and the fluorescence quenching spectra were recorded in the excitation wavelength of 330 nm at room temperature.

To validate the proposed method in a real sample, urine samples of healthy volunteers were collected for a recovery experiment and spiked with different concentrations of CTR standard solution. The urine samples were then centrifuged for 15 min, with 9000 rpm, and were diluted using water for ten times volume and filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. The results are summarized in Table 2, indicating satisfactory recovery results.

CDs size and structure were characterized by HRTEM. The chicken drumstick-derived CDs appeared spherical and also had quite a uniform structure (Fig. 1 A). The size distribution histogram of nanoparticles (inset) indicates that particle diameters were about 5 nm. Also, the TEM image of the chicken breast, chicken gizzard, and chicken feet CDs is shown in (Fig. S4). The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of the drumstick-derived CDs was recorded and exhibited a broad diffraction peak around 20.8°, which is attributed to the graphitic structure of the chicken drumstick CDs (Fig. S5).

The UV–Vis absorption of chicken drumstick CDs also exhibited an absorption peak at 280 nm, and the maximum emission band was at 417 nm once exited at 330 nm (Figure S6). The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of three other CDs were also shown in this figure. The fluorescence spectra of CDs excited at different wavelengths are shown in Fig. 1B, which were shifted to higher wavelengths in the range of 300 to 380 nm. The tunability of the emission spectra was based on the size-dependent fluorescence of CDs or the presence of different sites on the surface of CDs [8]. The inset figure in Fig. 1B shows the brilliant blue fluorescence emission of CDs (Filter paper impregnated with CDs) under UV light (λmax = 365 nm), and yellow color was then observed under daylight. Figure S7 shows the 3D fluorescence spectra of three other CDs. It is clear from Figure S8 that CDs derived from chicken drumsticks have a more brilliant fluorescence compared with others.

The FTIR of the prepared CDs derived from the chicken drumsticks, CTR, and CDs after the addition of CTR were also recorded to investigate the interaction between CD and CTR (Fig. 2 ). The peak at 3285 cm−1 in the IR spectrum of CDs exhibited a characteristic absorption band of O–H/N–H stretching vibrations, while the absorption bands of nonaliphatic C–H stretching vibrations appeared at 3090 cm−1. Moreover, two bands at 2947 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1were assigned to the C–H stretching vibrations. Also, the peak at 1668 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C = O due to the presence of carboxylic groups. The peak at 1550 cm−1 is attributed to C = C of aromatic circles. The presence of these functional groups suggested that the synthesized CDs had excellent water solubility. In the FTIR spectra of the CTR, the peaks at 3439 cm−1 and 3252 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of N–H. Also, the peak at 1648 cm−1 was ascribed to C = O stretching vibrations.

In the IR spectrum of CDs + CTR, the NH sharp bands that were in the IR spectra of CTR were emitted or deformed to broadband. The peak at 3285 cm−1 was converted to 3213 cm−1 and 3311 cm−1 in the IR spectra of CDs + CTR, which shows that the O–H groups have been affected. The peak of 1647 cm−1 in the spectrum of CTR was shifted to 1675 cm−1 in the IR spectra of CDs + CTR due to the O–H groups’ interaction. The 1743 cm−1 in the CTR spectra was also shifted to 1769 cm−1 and confirmed the reaction between the amino group of CTR and carboxylic acid groups of CDs.

The changes of carbonyl and O–H absorption bands, removed NH bands in the IR spectra of CDs + CTR confirmed that the CDs interact with CTR from N-H bands of CTR with CH aromatic bands and O–H of the CDs carboxylic acid group (Table S1).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to further analyze the chemical compositions, structure, and surface state of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a , three peaks at 283.0, 398.0, 530.0 eV were attributed to C1s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively [37]. Other peaks at 130.0, 151.0 and 162.0, belong to metal elements such as P 2p, S 2p, and Si 2P, respectively. The XPS results indicated that the as prepared CDs mainly contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements Fig. 4.

Specifically, in detail, the high-resolution spectrum of C1s spectrum in Fig. 3b exhibited three peaks at 283.2, 284.2, and 285.1 eV, which were attributed to C-OH/C-O, C–C, C-N/C-O bands, respectively. While the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) exhibited peaks attributed to N–H bonds. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s showed two peaks at 529.9 and 530.1 eV, which were attributed to C = O and C-O bands, respectively (Fig. 3d). Taken together, the XPS spectrum indicated that the synthesized CDs were functionalized with amino, carboxylic/carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups that confirmed the previous FTIR data. The presence of these functional groups imparts excellent solubility in water without further chemical modification.

Besides, QY was estimated for each CD synthesized from four various parts of the chicken. The corresponding standard curves are shown in (Figure S9). The QY of the chicken drumstick, chicken feet, chicken gizzard, and chicken breast were 32.86%, 26.55%, 26.38%,and 26.24%, respectively, Which is greater than the most previously reported carbon-based materials (Table1
). CHN analysis was also recorded to measure each parts’ carbon and nitrogen content that was shown in Table S2. According to the CHN analysis, the carbon and nitrogen percentage of the CDs synthesized from chicken drumstick is higher than the other 3 CDs. According to the previous reports, nitrogen improves the photoluminescence QYs of CDs [38–40]. Considering this fundamental reason, the QY of the chicken drumstick CDs is higher than others (Figure S10), so it was selected for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for the detection of CTR
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain optimum conditions in CTR detection, some factors of experimental parameters such as pH of CDs solutions, CDs concentrations, and reaction times were investigated. As shown in (Table S3) the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of CTR was maximum at pH 6.0. Then, the pH was adjusted at 6.0 for the determination of CTR using a phosphate buffer solution.

Different pHs can affect the functional groups at the CDs surface. In acidic pHs, the energy separation between the energy levels of the donor and acceptor increases. This increase in separation can interfere with the mechanism of electron-cavity and ultimately can reduce the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. It also reduces the fluorescence intensity. In base solution, COO− functional groups at the CDs surface can prevent electron coupling at the conduction level with electron deficiency cavities in the capacity layer by filling the electron deficiency cavities that result reducing of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs.

The investigation of reaction time between CTR and CDs also revealed that the quenching effect of CTR on fluorescence intensity was independent of reaction time. Therefore, CDs could be considered as fast fluorescence sensor for the determination of CTR without time control. Furthermore, the effect of CD concentration on the determination of CTR was investigated, and the optimum concentration was 100 ppm.

To investigate the CDs’ selectivity in the present study, the quenching effect of various drugs, cations, and amino acids on CDs fluorescence intensity was studied. As shown in Figure S11, different drugs such as vancomycin, acetaminophen, meropenem, ibuprofen, sulfanilamide, and cloxacillin had no or slight effects on the fluorescence of CDs. In contrast, fluorescence changes were significant upon the addition of CTR. Moreover, investigating the effect of different amino acids, and some cations on CDs fluorescence intensity showed that only Fe3+ affected significantly while the others had no or a slight change. The detailed sensing response of CDs toward CTR was studied and discussed further.

In optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured for the CTR determination. The findings revealed that the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs quenched as CTR concentrations increased. The relationship between the quenching of CDs fluorescence and CTR concentration could also be described by the Stern-Volmer equation as follows (Eq. (2)):
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 + K s v
[ Q ]
where KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, F0 and F respectively refer to fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the analyte, and [Q] represents analyte concentration. The Stern-Volmer plot showed a linear relationship between F0/F and the CTR concentration over the ranges 9 × 10−10 to 1.4 × 10−8 M (Fig. 3). Moreover, the LOD was estimated to be 4.4 × 10−10M (S/N = 3) for CTR (LOD = (3Sb/m)). Sb is the standard deviation of the blank and m is the slope of the calibration curve.
The comparison of the performances of this research with others is reported in Table 2. The present method was very selective and straightforward with a lower detection limit.

In order to explain the quenching mechanism of CTR by chicken drumstick CDs a series of investigations are carried out. As shown in Figure S12a the absorption peak of CTR was completely overlapped with the excitation wavelength of CDs. Consequently, the exciting light of CDs can be absorbed by CTR, which reduced the FL intensity of CDs. It suggests that the fluorescence quenching of the CDs may be from the IFE. IFE phenomenon is due to the absorption of the excitation or emission by absorbers in the detection system when the absorption spectra of the absorbers overlap with the excitation or emission spectra of CDs. To further confirm the primary reason for the FL quinching of CDs, corrections are made with (Eq.3):
(3)
F
cor
F
obs
=
10
A
ex
+
A
em
2
where Fobs refers to observed maximum fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected maximum fluorescence intensity by subtracting IFE from Fobs; Aex and Aem represent the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (λex = 330 nm) and maximum emission wavelength (λem = 417 nm), respectively. The corresponding calculated results were summarized in Table S4. Figure S12b shows the observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiencies (Eobs and Ecor) versus different concentrations of CTR. The results illustrated that approximately 95% of the quenching effect came from the IFE.

CTR concentrations in human urine samples were determined to demonstrate the reliability and applicability of the novel developed CDs. Furthermore, defined quantities of CTR were spiked to human urine samples to assess the analytical performance of CDs. The results were summarized in Table 3 , revealing that the sensor could be used to detect CTR with excellent accuracy in real samples. It should be noted that CTR was not recognized in any of the real samples before the experimental addition of CTR.

In summary, water-soluble, high-fluorescent, and blue-emitter CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of chickens’ meat. QY was estimated for each CD synthesized from four different parts of the chicken. In general, chicken drumstick CDs had the maximum QY compared with the other three CDs, and it was used for subsequent experiments. The proposed nanosensor was very selective towards the determination of CTR in contrast to other drugs and amino acids. This determination was based on fluorescence quenching of CDs when CTR was added. Therefore, CDs derived from chicken drumsticks were used for tracing CTR determination with low detection limits and satisfactory real sample results.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106372.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
